title: White sandwich loaf
subtitle: An everyday tin loaf, soft-crumbed and properly made
excerpt: A straight white sandwich loaf made with strong flour, a little butter for softness, and a long slow first rise that builds flavour. The sort of loaf that slices cleanly, toasts evenly, and keeps for three days without going stale.
sourceNotes: The basic white sandwich loaf follows the tradition documented in British household baking guides from the mid-nineteenth century onward, including Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, which records the standard milk-and-butter enriched tin loaf. The recipe here uses a slow room-temperature first rise rather than a fast warm-prove, following the approach favoured by Dan Lepard in Short and Sweet (2011) for a more developed crumb and better keeping quality.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice the cooled loaf and freeze flat in a bag for up to 2 months. Toast from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Shape the loaf and prove overnight in the fridge. Remove 30 minutes before baking.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven; drop to 190 degrees C after 15 minutes
baking.steamMethod: small roasting tin of boiling water on the oven floor for the first 10 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: A simple way to check whether a bread dough has been kneaded long enough. Stretch a small piece of dough between your fingers: if it holds a thin, semi-transparent membrane without tearing, gluten development is sufficient. If it tears immediately, return to kneading.

content.text: A good sandwich loaf requires nothing more than strong flour, water, yeast, salt, and a little butter. The enrichment keeps the crumb soft without making the loaf rich; the slow rise builds more flavour than a fast warm-prove. Knead until the dough passes the
, then give it time. This loaf rewards patience.

attrs.title: On oven temperature
attrs.body: Domestic ovens drift by 20 degrees C or more either side of the dial setting. An oven thermometer is worth using, especially for bread, where crust formation depends on a hot initial blast.

content.text: Combine 500 g strong white flour, 5 g instant yeast, 10 g salt, and 5 g sugar in a large bowl. Add 20 g softened butter and rub it into the flour until no large lumps remain. Make a well in the centre and pour in 300 ml lukewarm water. Mix to a rough dough, then turn out onto a lightly floured surface and knead for 8 to 10 minutes until the dough is smooth, elastic, and springs back when you press a finger into it. Test for the windowpane: stretch a small piece between your fingers. If it holds a thin membrane, the gluten is ready. If it tears, knead for another 2 minutes and test again.

Shape the dough into a ball and place in a lightly oiled bowl. Cover with cling film or a damp tea towel and leave at room temperature for around 90 minutes, until roughly doubled in size. The timing depends on the warmth of your kitchen: a cool room takes closer to 2 hours; a warm kitchen may be done in 60 minutes. Read the dough, not the clock.

Grease a 900 g (2 lb) loaf tin. Knock back the dough gently on a lightly floured surface, flatten it into a rectangle roughly the length of the tin, then roll it tightly into a cylinder and place it seam-side down in the tin. The dough should fill the tin to about two-thirds of its depth. Cover loosely and leave to prove for 45 to 60 minutes. The dough is ready when it crowns 1 to 2 cm above the rim of the tin and springs back slowly when you press a floured finger into it.

Heat the oven to 210 degrees C (conventional). Place a small roasting tin on the oven floor and pour in a cup of boiling water just before the loaf goes in. This steam softens the crust surface in the first 10 minutes and allows the loaf to spring open. Bake for 15 minutes at 210 degrees C, then drop to 190 degrees C and bake for a further 15 to 18 minutes. The loaf is done when the top is deep golden and the base sounds hollow when tapped. An internal probe should read 96 degrees C. Turn out onto a rack and leave to cool for at least 30 minutes before cutting.

items.problem: Loaf is dense with a tight crumb
items.solution: Under-proved, under-kneaded, or the yeast was inactive. The bulk ferment should double the dough; the prove should crown above the tin rim. Test the yeast before baking by dissolving it in warm water with a pinch of sugar and waiting 10 minutes for it to foam.
Oven temperature was too low, or the loaf was removed too early. Use an oven thermometer to verify the actual temperature. Bake until the base sounds hollow and the probe reads 96 degrees C.
Over-proved. When the prove goes too far, the gluten structure weakens and can't hold its shape in the oven. The dough should spring back slowly when pressed, not feel slack or billowy.
